title: Hazards of Immobility: Complications and Prevention
subtitle: An immobile, bedbound patient is at risk for complications in every body system. The nurse recognizes the hazards of immobility and applies prevention measures, then mobilizes the patient safely.

patient.name: Hazards of Immobility
patient.admitDate: A bedbound, immobile patient is at risk for system-wide complications. The nurse applies prevention measures and mobilizes safely.

nursesNote: An immobile, bedbound patient is at risk for complications in every body system. Respiratory: pooled secretions cause atelectasis and pneumonia. Cardiovascular: venous stasis causes deep vein thrombosis, and deconditioning causes orthostatic hypotension. Integumentary: pressure injuries form over bony prominences. Musculoskeletal: muscle atrophy, contractures, foot drop, and disuse osteoporosis. GI: constipation from slowed peristalsis. GU: urinary stasis with calculi and infection. The nurse prevents these by repositioning every 2 hours with skin assessment, encouraging incentive spirometry and deep breathing, applying sequential compression devices with ordered VTE prophylaxis, performing range-of-motion exercises, maintaining hydration and fiber, and — most importantly — mobilizing the patient as early and progressively as the condition allows. Position changes are made slowly to prevent orthostatic hypotension and falls.
providerOrders: Reposition every 2 hours and assess the skin
Encourage incentive spirometry and deep breathing while awake
Apply sequential compression devices and give ordered VTE prophylaxis
Perform range-of-motion exercises and maintain hydration and fiber
Mobilize early and progressively; change positions slowly to prevent orthostasis

opts.text: Pooled secretions raising the risk of pneumonia and atelectasis
Venous stasis raising the risk of deep vein thrombosis
Increased muscle mass and bone density from bed rest
Skin breakdown and pressure injuries over bony prominences
Constipation from slowed peristalsis
Improved orthostatic blood-pressure control
Repositioning every 2 hours helps prevent pressure injuries
Incentive spirometry and deep breathing reduce respiratory complications
Sequential compression devices and ambulation reduce DVT risk
Strict bed rest is the best way to prevent complications
Range-of-motion exercises help prevent contractures and atrophy
Changing positions slowly helps prevent orthostatic hypotension

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order getting an immobile patient up for the first time

opts.text: Assess strength, vital signs, and readiness
Raise the head of bed and let the patient sit upright
Dangle the legs at the bedside and check for dizziness
Assist to stand slowly with a gait belt and help
Ambulate or transfer to a chair, monitoring tolerance
Repositioning the patient every 2 hours
Keeping the patient flat and still all shift
Applying sequential compression devices
Encouraging hourly incentive spirometer use while awake
Withholding fluids to reduce urination
Performing range-of-motion exercises
Leaving the patient in one position for 6 hours
Early progressive mobilization

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Implement a prevention plan at the start of shift
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient's mobility status and risks
Reposition and perform a skin assessment
Apply SCDs and encourage incentive spirometry
Assist with range-of-motion and mobility as able
Document and update the mobility plan
Turning and repositioning the patient every 2 hours
Allowing the patient to lie in one position the entire shift
Encouraging deep breathing and incentive spirometry
Keeping the patient on strict bed rest when activity is allowed
Applying SCDs and giving ordered VTE prophylaxis
Standing the patient quickly without checking tolerance
Performing range-of-motion to prevent contractures
Restricting fluids and fiber in an immobile patient
